640020a88d3cf1e72e234741.question: This author lamented that “you must welcome from another part / Such noble moods as are not mine” in a poem that opens “I know I am but summer to your heart”. This author’s “Four Sonnets” include two that start “I think I should have loved you presently” and “I shall forget you presently my dear”. The entirety of a poem by this author is “Safe upon the solid rock, the ugly houses stand / Come and see my shining palace built upon the sand”. This author warned that “He whose soul is flat, the sky will cave in on him by and by”, and this poet claimed that (*) “Euclid alone has looked on Beauty bare”. This poet wrote that “All I could see from where I stood was three long mountains and a wood” in the poem “Renascence”. For 10 points, identify this author who wrote that “My candle burns brightly on both ends” in the collection A Few Figs from Thistles.

640020a88d3cf1e72e234745.question: One work by this artist shows a figure sitting in a dark room on the center panel while the outer panels show the same figure sitting on and bent-over a toilet. That work by this artist is one of his “black triptychs” painted after the suicide of his former model and lover. Another triptych by this artist was inspired by Aeschylus’ Oresteia and shows three amorphous figures in front of a (*) burnt-orange background. That painting is “Three Studies for Figures at the Base of a Crucifixion”. A work by this artist titled “Study for the Nurse in the Battleship Potemkin, 1957” is reminiscent of another work they modelled after a Diego Velazquez portrait of Pope Innocent X. For 10 points, name this British-Irish artist who painted a series of screaming popes.

640020a88d3cf1e72e234747.question: In a work by this author, the song “Hail! Hail! The gang’s all here” is sung at various points. In a play by this author, Booster is jailed for killing a woman, and his dad Becker struggles to keep his business alive. Harmond Wilks runs for mayor in a play by this author, who also killed off a recurring character at the age of 366 in the play King Hedley II. This author created the character of Aunt Ester, who lives at 1839 Wylie Avenue in the play (*) Gem of the Ocean. The title object of one of this author’s plays was stolen from the Sutters by their slaves, and frames an argument between Boy Willie and Berniece over its sale. This author also wrote a play in which Rose’s husband is unfaithful. For 10 points, identify this author of The Piano Lesson who created the garbage collector Troy Maxson in Fences.

640020a88d3cf1e72e234783.leadin_sanitized: For 10 points each, answer some questions about The Critique of Pure Reason:
parts: The Critique of Pure Reason is often referred to as the first critique by this Prussian author, who also wrote The Critique of Practical Reason and The Critique of the Power of Judgement.

In the preface to the B-edition of The Critique, Kant explains that his project is to explore the possibility that instead of our intuition conforming to objects, objects conform to our faculty of intuition. He explains that move with an analogy to this astronomer who now names that “Hypothesis” of Kant’s.

In the B-Edition of The Critique, Kant includes a ‘refutation’ of a ‘problematic’ form of this philosophical position which he characterizes as the view that the existence of external objects is doubtful or false. Kant’s philosophy is often called a “transcendental” form of this philosophy, of which George Berkeley is a prime example.

Kant's philosophy is often called a "transcendental" form of this philosophy, of which George Berkeley is a prime example.

640020a88d3cf1e72e2347f8.leadin: For 10 points each, answer some questions about a famous artist:
640020a88d3cf1e72e2347f8.leadin_sanitized: For 10 points each, answer some questions about a famous artist:
parts: This German artist’s watercolour works include one depicting a young hare and another titled “The Large Piece of Turf”, but they’re perhaps better known for engravings such as “Melencolia I” and “Knight, Death, and the Devil”.
Another notable engraving by Dürer is this one, which depicts the author of the Vulgate bent over his desk while a lion and dog sleep in front of him.

In 1515 Dürer made a woodcut depicting one of these animals which hadn’t been seen alive in Europe since Roman times. Dürer’s depiction gives the animal skin like plated armor and was generally wacky enough for Salvador Dali to make a sculpture of it.

This event leads to the death of Phineus, who was earlier engaged to one of the central figures of this event. Cepheus and (*) Cassiopeia were the parents of one of the two central figures at this event, while the other still had winged sandals from Hermes. 640020a88d3cf1e72e234852.leadin: Despite the fact that initially only he received credit for their work, they flew in separate planes so the other could complete their work in case one died. For 10 points each, answer some questions about Christo and Jeanne-Claude.
640020a88d3cf1e72e234852.leadin_sanitized: Despite the fact that initially only he received credit for their work, they flew in separate planes so the other could complete their work in case one died. For 10 points each, answer some questions about Christo and Jeanne-Claude.
parts: A series of works by Christo and Jeanne-Claude involve wrapping things in various fabrics. They’ve done this to landmarks such as the Pont-neuf and this German parliament building, whose glass dome was designed by Norman Foster.

Another project by Christo and Jeanne-Claude was the installation of thousands of these objects in Japan and California. 1,340 blue ones were installed in Japan while 1,760 yellow ones were installed in California. They had to removed when one toppled and killed a person.

For the artwork The Gates, Christo and Jeanne-Claude installed over 7,500 vinyl gates in this city. Other installation pieces in this city include Richard Serra’s Tilted Arc and a room containing over 250 cubic yards of dirt.
parts_sanitized: A series of works by Christo and Jeanne-Claude involve wrapping things in various fabrics. They've done this to landmarks such as the Pont-neuf and this German parliament building, whose glass dome was designed by Norman Foster.

Another project by Christo and Jeanne-Claude was the installation of thousands of these objects in Japan and California. 1,340 blue ones were installed in Japan while 1,760 yellow ones were installed in California. They had to removed when one toppled and killed a person.

For the artwork The Gates, Christo and Jeanne-Claude installed over 7,500 vinyl gates in this city. Other installation pieces in this city include Richard Serra's Tilted Arc and a room containing over 250 cubic yards of dirt.

The Chronic Argonauts has this conceit, which is also central to the troubles caused by Scorpius in a 2016 play partially titled for a (*) “cursed child”. The cryptic phrase “three turns ought to do it” is an instruction to bring about this development in order to save Buckbeak. parts: This French artist is famous for sculptures such as The Kiss, The Gates of Hell, and one showing a seated man with his hand under his chin titled, The Thinker.
Rodin depicted this person dressed in a robe in one monument which was rejected by its commissioner, and other works by Rodin show this man with folded arms and completely naked.

Rodin often made sculptures after this lover of his, who was a notable sculptor in her own right. This sculptor is known for works such as The Waltz, and a bronze and onyx statue of three women playing, called The Wave.
